topic: Isfahan Steel Mill (Aryamehr / National Iranian Steel Corporation)
detail: Isfahan chosen; availability of water was the prime consideration, plus central location relative to raw materials (Kerman-area coal/iron ore) and markets (concentrated in Tehran area)

Financed via a gas-sales arrangement (barter) with the Soviet Union -- natural gas exports as repayment, attractive partly because no alternative export market for the gas existed at the time (LNG liquefaction not yet commercially developed)
USSR was reportedly the ONLY country prepared to supply an integrated iron and steelworks to Iran at the time of planning, limiting the Government's choice
Aryashar -- a new modern town built alongside the steel complex to serve it; construction cost of the town was NOT initially separated from the steel plant's cost in official accounting

750,000 tons of ingot steel by mid-1972 (planned output later limited to this figure rather than a more economically efficient 1.2 million tons, due to a binding constraint: limited supply of high-quality coking coal and water for coal washeries at the mines -- required somewhat uneconomic coal-mining procedures to hit the startup date)
2 to 2.5 million tons by 1975
4 to 5 million tons by the end of the 1970s
Infrastructure at Isfahan estimated adequate for a mill of up to 7 million tons, though planning beyond 4-5 million tons was 'not yet advanced' as of this 1972 report

Conventional blast furnace with gas fuel injection, coke ovens and auxiliary by-product plant, L.D. (Linz-Donawitz) steel converters, continuous casting equipment, and rolling mills; bars/rods (light to heavy sections) rolled up to 2 million ton capacity, flat-rolled products envisaged only once capacity exceeds 2 million tons

Construction running according to schedule -- described as 'a formidable achievement' given enterprise size, attributed to successful collaboration between Iranian civil engineers in charge and their Russian technical advisers

Report states it was 'impossible accurately to assess the investment' for the first-stage plant specifically, because much of the initial investment was already made in preparation for the larger 2-million-plus-ton stages, and infrastructure/town costs were commingled with plant costs in the accounts -- no single all-in dollar figure given in this source

item: Competitive positioning risk noted by report
detail: Report flags that the Isfahan mill's initial product mix (bars/rods) would compete directly with existing private bar-rolling mills near Ahwaz, and notes it was 'perhaps unfortunate' the Isfahan mill did not begin with flat-rolled products given larger unmet demand for those

topic: IDRO (Industrial Development and Renovation Organization) capital-goods plants
detail: Location: Arak. Partner: Reynolds (US) -- matches the 'Iralco' aluminum smelter also documented independently in the USGS Minerals Yearbook 1975 chapter (same round, see usgs-minerals-yearbook/historical-pre1990-volumes/iran-production-extraction/)
Location: Arak. Same financing arrangement as the steel complex (i.e. USSR barter financing)
Location: Tabriz. Financed and technically assisted by Czechoslovakia
Location: Tabriz. Financed and technically assisted by Romania

All (except the aluminum plant) described as 'very solid construction' but 'lacking in flexibility'; all being completed on schedule and locally managed; report flags a forward risk that combined production capacity of these plants will exceed local product demand for some time, making IDRO's product-mix design and export strategy important for economic multi-shift utilization

'Chit' and 'khodari' mass-consumption textiles -- at least 70 percent of total domestic textile consumption -- priced approximately 25-40 percent above the average price of similar products imported into the USA (net of indirect taxes); wool prices (net of high import duties on wool) roughly comparable to import prices

Selected automobile makes in Iran priced 15-40 percent above international prices; the Paykan specifically -- described as having 'the largest market' -- was only about 15 percent above the price set by a recently-determined international competitive tender in Kuwait, i.e. the most internationally price-competitive Iranian-made car of the ones compared
Roughly comparable in price to imports, attributed to the high proportion of labor cost in bus production

Import substitution being extended to motor-vehicle COMPONENT industries specifically (as opposed to just final assembly) -- new intermediate-product plants planned/underway for piston, gearbox and electrical-systems production to service the motor vehicle industry; a compressor plant likewise planned to service the refrigerator industry

Capital goods production 'presently very small' -- chief components: trucks, buses, trailers, communications equipment, and water pumps. New IDRO-linked public enterprises were adding machine tools, agricultural machinery and other engineering products
